perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, claims, demands and other communications hereunder will be given in writing and will be deemed given and effective on the earliest of (i) the date of transmission, if such notice or communication is delivered via facsimile or e-mail of a .pdf, .tif, .gif, .jpeg or similar electronic attachment at the facsimile number or email address, respectively, specified for the relevant receiving Party before 5:30 p.m. (in the time zone of the receiving Party) on a business day (in the time zone of the receiving Party), (ii) the next business day after the date of transmission, if such notice or communication is delivered via facsimile or e-mail of a .pdf, .tif, .gif, .jpeg or similar electronic attachment at the facsimile number or email address, respectively, specified for the relevant receiving Party on a day that is not a business day or later than 5:30 p.m. on any business day (in each case, in the time zone of the receiving Party), (iii) one business day following the date of deposit with a nationally recognized overnight courier service for delivery on the following business day, or (iv) upon actual receipt by the party to whom such notice is required to be given, but if delivery is not accepted, then on the date delivery is refused.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, claims, demands and other communications hereunder will be given in writing and will be deemed given and effective only upon actual receipt by the party to whom such notice is required to be given, even if delivery is refused.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice clause to be effective ONLY upon actual receipt, even if refused, contradicts common legal standards and could violate California Civil Code regarding notification, especially in scenarios like contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1587 states that an acceptance may be revoked before its delivery is communicated to the offeror. This clause, requiring actual receipt even if delivery is refused, effectively nullifies the right to revoke acceptance before communication, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1587 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Consent [1565 - 1590] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1587. A proposal is revoked by any of the following: (a) By the communication of notice of revocation by the proposer to the other party, in the manner prescribed by Sections 1581 and 1583, before his or her acceptance has been communicated to the former. (b) By the lapse of the time prescribed in the proposal for its acceptance or, if no time is prescribed, the lapse of a reasonable time without communication of the acceptance. (c) By the failure of the acceptor to fulfill a condition precedent to acceptance. (d) By the death or legal incapacity to make decisions of the proposer. (Amended by Stats. 2014, Ch. 144, Sec. 2. (AB 1847) Effective January 1, 2015.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the notice clause to be effective only upon actual receipt, even if refused. This directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1587, which allows for the revocation of a proposal before its acceptance is communicated to the offeror. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'A proposal is revoked by any of the following: (a) By the communication of notice of revocation by the proposer to the other party...before his or her acceptance has been communicated to the former.' By mandating actual receipt regardless of refusal, the modified clause effectively prevents the revocation of acceptance before communication, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereunder, each Party agrees that, for a period of six months from the date of this Agreement, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment with such Party or any of its Affiliates, any of the officers or employees of the other Party or its Affiliates (a) with whom such Party has had contact during the evaluation of a Possible Transaction or (b) who were identified by name and function to such Party by an officer or employee of the other Party with whom such Party or its Representatives had contact during the evaluation of a Possible Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereunder, each Party agrees that, for a period of two years from the date of this Agreement, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment with such Party or any of its Affiliates, any of the officers or employees of the other Party or its Affiliates (a) with whom such Party has had contact during the evaluation of a Possible Transaction or (b) who were identified by name and function to such Party by an officer or employee of the other Party with whom such Party or its Representatives had contact during the evaluation of a Possible Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation agreement to two years may be deemed overly broad and unreasonable under California law, potentially violating the Business and Professions Code.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow restraints are permitted, a two-year non-solicitation agreement may be considered an unlawful restraint of trade if it is deemed overly broad or unreasonable in its scope or duration, especially if it unduly restricts an employee's ability to find employment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from six months to two years. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this extension may violate California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The two-year non-solicitation agreement could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within the State of Delaware, without regard to the conflict of law provisions that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Korea applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within the State of North Korea, without regard to the conflict of law provisions that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting North Korean law as the governing law is against public policy, especially when the agreement is between a Delaware corporation and a company in India, with activities occurring in California. This choice would likely be unenforceable in any US court due to fundamental differences in legal principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose the law that governs their contract, but that choice is not absolute. The choice is invalid if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or if application of the chosen law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue and which would be the state of the applicable law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties. Here, North Korea has no reasonable relationship, and applying North Korean law violates fundamental US public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text selects North Korean law as the governing law, which directly contradicts the principles of Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation details that the choice of law is invalid if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or transaction and applying that law would violate fundamental US public policy. Given that the original agreement involves a Delaware corporation and a company in India with activities in California, North Korea has no reasonable relationship, and its legal system fundamentally differs from US standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions, implying the necessity of a reasonable connection, which North Korea lacks. This change introduces a clear legal contradiction as it selects a law that is unlikely to be enforceable in any US court and goes against public policy.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all obligations will terminate three years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all obligations will terminate 30 days from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the term of the NDA to 30 days for confidential information potentially violates California's Trade Secret Law if the confidential information retains its value and secrecy beyond that period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Trade Secret Law protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the NDA to 30 days may not provide adequate protection for legitimate trade secrets, potentially violating the law if misappropriation occurs after the 30-day period. The agreement's short duration fails to provide reasonable protection, especially if the confidential information requires a longer protection period to maintain its value and secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the NDA term to 30 days, which potentially contradicts California's Trade Secret Law. The Law Explanation states that this short duration may not adequately protect trade secrets, especially if they require a longer protection period to maintain their value. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Code, CIV 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Perturbation Explanation states that reducing the NDA term may violate California's Trade Secret Law if the confidential information retains its value and secrecy beyond 30 days. Therefore, the change sets a deadline that potentially violates state law, fitting the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.
